front: Figure: his presidential library is located in Hyde Park, New York
work: General / Giveaway Identifiers

front: Figure: a memorial to him in Washington D.C.'s Tidal Basin features George Segal sculptures of Depression-era Americans listening to a radio and waiting in a breadline
work: General / Giveaway Identifiers

front: Place: island in the East River, formerly called Welfare Island, renamed for this president
back: Franklin D. Roosevelt Island (Franklin Delano Roosevelt)
work: General / Giveaway Identifiers

front: Place: Washington State reservoir formed by the Grand Coulee Dam, named for this president
work: General / Giveaway Identifiers

front: Figure: the only U.S. president elected to more than two terms
work: General / Giveaway Identifiers

front: Figure: as Assistant Secretary of the Navy under Josephus Daniels, he helped establish the North Sea Mine Barrage to stop German U-boats
work: Early Career and Path to the Presidency

front: Figure: ran unsuccessfully for vice president in 1920 as James Cox's running mate
work: Early Career and Path to the Presidency

front: Figure: as president, rewarded his old Navy boss Josephus Daniels by appointing him U.S. Ambassador to Mexico
work: Early Career and Path to the Presidency

front: Figure: advised by an original "Brain Trust" of Columbia professors including Rexford Tugwell and Adolf Berle
work: The New Deal: Relief, Recovery, and Reform

front: Figure: advisor Harry Hopkins ran this president's Works Progress Administration and later served as his primary wartime emissary to Winston Churchill
work: The New Deal: Relief, Recovery, and Reform

front: Figure: Frances Perkins, the first woman appointed to a presidential Cabinet, served as this president's Secretary of Labor
work: The New Deal: Relief, Recovery, and Reform

front: Law: 1935 act creating a national old-age pension system, later upheld against a benefits-eligibility challenge in Flemming v. Nestor
work: The New Deal: Relief, Recovery, and Reform

front: Law: New Deal act signed under this president's second vice president, Henry A. Wallace
work: The New Deal: Relief, Recovery, and Reform

front: Case: struck down the National Industrial Recovery Act's "straight killing" codes for meat producers, nicknamed the "sick chicken" case
back: Schechter Poultry Corp. v. United States (Franklin Delano Roosevelt)
work: The New Deal: Relief, Recovery, and Reform

front: Institution: expanded the National Park Service under this president through this conservation program, headed by Robert Fechner
work: The New Deal: Relief, Recovery, and Reform

front: Figure: radio priest Charles Coughlin initially supported but then turned against this president, calling his work programs "Christ's Deal" before saying America could choose him "or Ruin"
work: The New Deal: Relief, Recovery, and Reform

front: Event: this president's plan to add up to six new Supreme Court justices failed in Congress in part after ally Joseph Taylor Robinson died of a heart attack during the fight
work: Court-Packing Plan and the Supreme Court

front: Event: "Black Monday" was a Supreme Court defeat and "White Monday" a victory for this president's agenda
work: Court-Packing Plan and the Supreme Court

front: Event: the Supreme Court's turn toward upholding this president's New Deal policies was signaled by Justice Owen Roberts's "switch in time that saved nine"
work: Court-Packing Plan and the Supreme Court

front: Case: denied this president's authority to fire a Federal Trade Commission member, argued by the executors of William Humphrey's estate
back: Humphrey's Executor v. United States (Franklin Delano Roosevelt)
work: Court-Packing Plan and the Supreme Court

front: Event: speculation over whether this president would break with two-term tradition led cartoonists to depict him as the Sphinx
back: Franklin Delano Roosevelt running for a third term
work: The Election of 1940 and the Third Term

front: Event: this president's decision to seek a third term sank the political ambitions of James Farley and John Nance Garner and later prompted passage of the 22nd Amendment
back: Franklin Delano Roosevelt running for a third term
work: The Election of 1940 and the Third Term

front: Event: defeated Republican Wendell Willkie in the 1940 election to win a third term
back: Franklin Delano Roosevelt running for a third term
work: The Election of 1940 and the Third Term

front: Event: George Gallup's newly-founded polling company made its name by correctly predicting this president would defeat Alf Landon in 1936
work: The Election of 1940 and the Third Term

front: Event: helped codify the declarative theory of statehood at this 1933 international conference, attended by female delegate Sophonisba Breckinridge
work: Good Neighbor Policy and Latin America

front: Event: this president's Latin American outreach policy commissioned the Walt Disney film Saludos Amigos, in which singer Carmen Miranda's work was later criticized
work: Good Neighbor Policy and Latin America

front: Event: his administration drafted Haiti's constitution and ended the U.S. occupation of the country
work: Good Neighbor Policy and Latin America

front: Institution: began this program admitting Mexican farmworkers to remedy a wartime labor shortage; his successor ended it
work: Good Neighbor Policy and Latin America

front: Figure: Secretary of State Cordell Hull sent Walt Disney on a tour of South America under this president's Good Neighbor policy
work: Good Neighbor Policy and Latin America

front: Event: this president was in office during Japan's 1937 attack on the American gunboat USS Panay
work: Path to World War II

front: Concept: proposed constitutional amendment, debated after the Panay incident, that would have required a national referendum before any declaration of war
work: Path to World War II

front: Document: sent to this president in August 1939, written largely by Leo Szilard but signed by Albert Einstein, urging atomic bomb research before the Nazis could develop one
work: Path to World War II

front: Figure: supported the Quit India movement and pressed Winston Churchill to accommodate some of Mohandas Gandhi's demands while sending Britain Lend-Lease aid
work: Path to World War II

front: Law: signed by this president, this act granted the Philippines a ten-year path to independence before Japan captured the colony during World War II
work: Path to World War II

front: Event: roughly thirty evening radio addresses, typically opening with "My friends" and followed by a performance of the "Star-Spangled Banner"
work: Fireside Chats

front: Event: the first of these addresses was delivered on the bank holiday this president had proclaimed, explaining in simple terms how banks worked
work: Fireside Chats

front: Event: one of these addresses urged Americans to help resist the Nazis by serving as the "Arsenal of Democracy"
work: Fireside Chats

front: Figure: CBS reporter who is said to have coined the common name for these radio addresses, whose format was partly originated by press secretary Stephen Early
work: Fireside Chats

front: Figure: speechwriter who claimed some fireside chats were ghostwritten by Charles Michelson and Donald Richberg
work: Fireside Chats

front: Event: directly inspired the televised address Jimmy Carter delivered while wearing a wool cardigan
work: Fireside Chats

front: Institution: this president's informal group of 45 African American advisors included Robert C. Weaver and National Youth Administration official Mary McLeod Bethune
work: The Black Cabinet and Civil Rights

front: Institution: created by this president to expand the role of Black soldiers in the military and address workplace discrimination
back: Fair Employment Practice Committee (Franklin Delano Roosevelt)
work: The Black Cabinet and Civil Rights

front: Institution: established as part of the Federal Writers' Project under this president
work: The Black Cabinet and Civil Rights

front: Figure: this president's wife took a heavily publicized flight with C. Alfred Anderson, the "Father of Black Aviation"
work: The Black Cabinet and Civil Rights

front: Figure: a 1994 book recalled his continued correspondence with Lucy Mercer Rutherfurd despite his wife's opposition, and debated whether he had an affair with private secretary Missy LeHand
work: Personal Life, Legacy, and Cultural Depictions

front: Document: Doris Kearns Goodwin conducted 86 interviews for this book about this president and his wife
work: Personal Life, Legacy, and Cultural Depictions

front: Figure: called "the soldier of freedom" and "the lion and the fox" in biographies by James MacGregor Burns
work: Personal Life, Legacy, and Cultural Depictions

front: Figure: composer Charles Ives, at age 63, sent this president a personal letter proposing a "People's World Union"
work: Personal Life, Legacy, and Cultural Depictions

front: Figure: this president's funeral set the precedent for playing Samuel Barber's Adagio for Strings at somber public occasions
work: Personal Life, Legacy, and Cultural Depictions

front: Work: artist Elizabeth Shoumatoff was painting this at Warm Springs, Georgia when this president died, and later replicated it largely from memory
work: Personal Life, Legacy, and Cultural Depictions
